If I had taken the safe job, I would not be here now.
If we had not played the long game, we would not have this reputation today.
If she were not so honest, she would have hidden the mistake.
If those choices had been different, everything would be different now.
If I did not believe in slow growth, I would never have turned it down.
Eliane, I have been thinking about the early days. Do you ever wonder where you would be if you had taken that hospital job?
All the time. If I had taken it, I would not be running this clinic now. That refusal was the defining moment.
It really was the pivotal one. Half the room thought you were making a mistake.
I know. But that decision became the cornerstone the whole practice was built on.
And the course correction in year three? Closing that service could not have been easy.
It was hard-won. But if I were not willing to admit mistakes early, I would have carried that failure for years.
One choice, and the ripple effect on everything after it -- the people, the research, the reputation.
Exactly. I played the long game, and now the work has come full circle.
That is the real legacy, Eliane. Not the building -- the decisions that still shape everyone here.
That decision was the cornerstone the whole clinic was built on.
It was a hard-won course correction, but it saved the team.
I played the long game, and the work has come full circle.
The real legacy is the decisions that still shape everyone here.

Eliane, it's Adrian. I was going through the old board minutes tonight and it stopped me. Do you remember the meeting where you told us you were turning down the hospital post? Half the room thought you were making a mistake. Looking back, that was the pivotal one. If you had listened to us back then, you would be a comfortable department head somewhere, and none of this would exist. That refusal was the cornerstone of everything that followed. And the course correction in year three, closing that service, that was hard-won, but if you hadn't had the honesty to do it, we would still be losing money today. I just wanted you to hear someone say it out loud: the present you built came from those decisions. Anyway, call me back. I would like to talk about the long game for the next five years.

When residents ask me how the clinic came to be, I don't start with the building. I start with a decision. Years ago, I turned down a safe hospital post, and if I had taken it, I would not be standing here now. That one choice was formative; it became the defining moment everything else was built on. It compounded in ways I never planned: it had a ripple effect that brought the right people to me, and a knock-on effect on our research and our reputation. There was one hard-won course correction along the way, and honestly, if I weren't willing to admit mistakes early, I would have wasted years. I played the long game, and slowly the work has come full circle. The real legacy isn't the clinic; it's the decisions that still shape everyone who walks through that door.
